content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 867 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statements of Economic Interest
(916) 322 5662        322 5660           322 5901          322 6441              327 6444

January 25, 1983

Ms. Gail Hutton
City Attorney
P.O. Box 190
2000 Main Street
Huntington Beach, CA 92648

Re: Your Request for Advice, Our
     Advice No. A-82-210

Dear Ms. Hutton:

     Thank you for your letter regarding the conflict of
interests provisions of the Political Reform Act. You stated in
your letter that Councilman Ron Pattinson is an employee of a
company wholly owned by Councilman John Thomas. This company,
John A. Thomas Crane and Trucking, does work for Huntington
Beach Company, a major land owner in the city that frequently
has development projects before the Council.1/  Based on these
facts, you asked whether Councilman Pattinson and Councilman
Thomas must disqualify themselves from governmental decisions
affecting Thomas Crane & Trucking or Huntington Beach Company.

     Government Code Section 87100 2/ prohibits an official from
making and participating in the making of a governmental
decision in which he knows or has reason to know he has a
financial interest. An official has a financial interest in a
decision within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably
foreseeable that a decision will have a material financial
effect3/ on:

_________________________

     1/  The Huntington Beach Company paid John A. Thomas Crane
and Trucking over $10,000 in 1981.

     2/  Hereinafter all references are to the Government Code
unless otherwise indicated.

     3/  The phrase "material financial effect" is explained in
2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18702.

Ms. Gail Hutton
January 25, 1983
Page Two


     (a)  Any business entity in which the public official
has an investment worth more than $1,000.

     (b)  Any real property in which the public official has
an interest worth more than $1,000.

     (c)  Any source of income aggregating $250 or more
received within 12 months prior to the time when the
decision is made.

     (d)  Any business entity in which the public official
is an employee or holds any position of management.

     Councilman Thomas has an investment interest of over $1,000
in Thomas Crane and Trucking; it is a source of income to him of
over $250; and he holds a position of management in the business
entity.  The company is also a source of income to its employee,
Mr. Pattinson.  Thus, neither Mr. Thomas nor Mr. Pattinson can
make or participate in the making of a governmental decision if
it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a
material financial effect on Thomas Crane and Trucking.

     Huntington Beach Company is also a source of income over
$250 to Councilman Thomas.  Since Mr. Thomas is sole owner of
Thomas Crane & Trucking, income from Huntington Beach Company to
the trucking company is deemed to be personal income to
Mr. Thomas.4/  Therefore Mr. Thomas must abstain from any
decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that it will have a
material financial effect on the Huntington Beach Company.

     I hope I have clarified your questions about the Political
Reform Act.  If you, or the Councilmembers, have any additional
questions, please feel free to contact me at 916/322-5901.

                                    Very truly yours,

                                    [signature]
                                    Janis Shank McLean
                                    Counsel
                                    Legal Division

JSM:km
Enclosures

_________________________

     4/  Section 82030(a) provides that income of an individual
includes a pro rata share of any income of any business entity
in which the individual owns a 10% interest or greater.

OFFICE OF

CITY ATTORNEY

P. O. BOX 190
2000 MAIN STREET
HUNTINGTON BEACH
CALIFORNIA 92648

GAIL HUTTON
City Attorney

TELEPHONE
(714) 536-5555

December 14, 1982

Fair Political Practices Commission
Legal Division
1100 "K" Street
Post Office Box 807
Sacramento, California 95804

Gentlemen:

The City of Huntington Beach requests an opinion on the possible
conflict of interest arising from one council member working for
a corporation owned by another.

As we understand the facts, Councilman Ron Pattinson (employee)
works for a company wholly owned by Councilman John Thomas
(employer). The company is John A. Thomas Crane and Trucking,
Inc., a California corporation. Employee has no interest in the
company. There is no suggestion of any coercion by the employer.

The company does some work for the Huntington Beach Company, a
corporation of which Standard Oil of California is the majority
shareholder. We do not know the dollar amount of this work or
the relationship it bears to the Thomas company's annual income.
The Huntington Beach Company is a major landowner in the city and
frequently has development projects before the Council. We also
generally do not have specific knowledge whether any particular
project has a material financial effect on the Huntington Beach
Company or its parent corporation. Thomas reports income of over
$10,000 to the Thomas company from the Huntington Beach Company
on Schedule 72lH; Pattinson does not list any income from the
Huntington Beach Company on his conflict of interest disclosure
statement.

We have written an opinion analyzing this problem and attach a
copy. In general, we view §87103(d) as barring both employer and
employee if a decision has a material financial effect on the
Thomas company. This could occur if it was reasonably foreseeable that a decision would result in work contracts for the
Thomas company that have a significant effect upon that entity
but §87103(c) only bars the employer if the material effect is on
the Huntington Beach Company as a source of income to Thomas or
his company. The employee is not disqualified in every situation merely because the employer is disqualified.

Fair Political Practices Commission
December 14, 1982
Page Two

We would appreciate your thoughts on the accuracy of our opinion.

Please contact us if you need any further information.

Very truly yours,

[signature]
GAIL HUTTON
City Attorney

GH:RCS:ps
Enclosure
cc: Mayor and Members of City Council
    City Administrator

CITY OF HUNTINGTON BEACH
INTER-DEPARTMENT COMMUNICATION

TO COUNCILMAN PATTINSON                 FROM GAIL HUTTON
                                              City Attorney

Subject CONFLICTS OF INTEREST AS AN          Date December 8, 1982
        EMPLOYEE OF A BUSINESS ENTITY

MEMORANDUM OPINION

We have been asked to render an opinion on situations where a
councilman may be in a conflict of interest by virtue of his
status as an employee of a business entity owned by another
councilman. Rather than address specific fact situations, we
will speak generally of the applicable principles.

The touchstone of the conflict of interest law is Government Code
§87100:

        "No public official at any level of state or
        local government shall make, participate in
        making or in any way attempt to use his
        official position to influence a governmental
        decision in which he knows or has reason to
        know he has a financial interest."

An official has a "financial interest" if it is reasonably fore-
seeable that the decision will have a material financial effect,
distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, upon any
of the following interests:

        "(a) Any business entity in which the public
        official has a direct or indirect investment
        worth more than one thousand dollars ($1,000)

        "(b) Any real property in which the public
        official has a direct or indirect interest
        worth more than one thousand dollars ($1,000)

        "(c) Any source of income, other than loans
        by a commercial lending institution in the
        regular course of business on terms available
        to the public without regard to official
        status, aggregating two hundred fifty dollars
        ($250) or more in value provided to, received
        by or promised to the public official within
        12 months prior to the time when the decision
        is made.

Councilman Pattinson                                December 8, 1982
Re: Conflict of Interest                                    Page Two

"(d) Any business entity in which the
public official is a director, officer,
partner, trustee, employee, or holds any
position of management."

Government Code §87103

It is obvious from reading these categories that not all of them
apply to an employee of another councilmember. Interests that
might disqualify the employer under paragraphs (a) and (b) will
not necessarily disqualify the employee, unless he is also an
investor. Likewise, paragraph (c) will not generally apply to
the employee except as paragraph (d) applies. Thus, the mere
fact that the employer is disqualified by a conflict of interest
under these paragraphs does not disqualify the employee.

The pertinent limitation is (d), a foreseeable material financial
effect upon a business entity of which the councilmember is an
employee. Such a material effect would disqualify the council-
member. (See Witt v. Morrow (1977) 70 Cal.App.3d 817, 139
Cal.Rptr. 161).

The conflict of interest law does not define "material financial
effect." However, the regulations of the FPPC set forth various
tests.

Under the regulations, a decision has a material effect only if
it will have a "significant effect" on the business entity. The
FPPC summarizes the situations defining whether it is foreseeable
whether a decision will be significant in its pamphlet explaining
the act:

"As a general rule, an effect of
$100,000 or more on real property or a
business entity . . . is considered
material. When the effect of a govern-
mental decision is between $1,000 and
$100,000 the effect may be material."

The applicable regulation lists the following factors in con-
sidering whether the effect upon the business entity employing
the councilmember may foreseeably be a material financial effect:

"In determining whether it is reasonably
foreseeable that the effects of a
governmental decision will be
significant . . ., consideration should
be given to the following factors:

Councilman Pattinson                                    December 8, 1982
Re: Conflict of Interest                                         Page Three

     "Whether, in the case of a business
entity . . . (or source of income) in which
the public official is a director, officer,
partner, employee, trustee or holds any
position of management, the effect of the
decision will be to increase or decrease:
     (A) The annualized gross revenues by the
lesser of:
          1. One hundred thousand dollars
($100,000); or
          2. One percent if it is one thousand
dollars ($1,000) or more; or
     (B) Annual net income by the lesser of:
          1. Fifty thousand dollars ($50,000);
          2. One half of one percent if it is
one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more;
or
     (C) Current assets or liabilities by the
lesser of:
          1. One hundred thousand dollars
($100,000); or
          2. One half of one percent if it is
one thousand dollars or more.

     "Current assets are deemed to be decreased by
the amount of any expenses incurred as a
result of a governmental decision."

(2 California Administrative Code §18702)

     It can be readily seen that it is a question of fact whether a
particular decision will have a material financial effect on the
business entity and requires knowledge and analysis of the income
and assets of the business that may not be public information and
is beyond the scope of the disclosure requirements.

     The focus of the inquiry in analyzing decisions that affect a
business entity employing a council member is the financial
effect on that business entity. If the employer council member
is disqualified under §87103(d), the employee council member
should also be disqualified. However, this should not be con-
fused with the "source of income" effect upon an applicant before
the Council. Section 87103(c) provides that an official has a
financial interest if the decision will have a foreseeably
material financial effect upon any source of income aggregating
$250 or more to the official. In the case of the employee
council member, the "source of income" will normally be the

Councilman Pattinson
Re: Conflict of Interest

December 8, 1982
Page Four

employing business entity. However, with the employer council
member, the "source of income" will normally be the applicant
before the Council. Thus, the mere fact that the employer is
disqualified under the "source of income" rule does not per se
require disqualification of the employee.

For example, assume a developer has a subdivision map before the
Council. Developer has paid $250 to the employer councilman, but
has not paid anything to the employee councilman. The "source of
income" inquiry is whether the decision will have a material
financial effect upon the developer; if so, employer is dis-
qualified, but not employee. Only if the decision additionally
has material financial effect upon the employing business
entity is the employee council member disqualified.

These are the general guidelines. If we are provided with
specific fact situations and figures, we will attempt to apply
the above principles and render advisory opinions.

[signature]
GAIL HUTTON
City Attorney

GH:RCS:ps/[illegible]

sections.question: s, please teel tree to contact me at 916/322—5901.
sections.conclusion: Neither Mr. Thomas nor Mr. Pattinson can make or participate in the making of a governmental decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on Thomas Crane and Trucking. Therefore Mr. Thomas must abstain from any decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that it will have a material financial effect on the Huntington Beach Company.
sections.question_synthetic: Must two city councilmen disqualify themselves from governmental decisions when one is an employee of a company wholly owned by the other, and that company does work for a major landowner that frequently brings development projects before the council?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Both councilmen are disqualified from decisions with reasonably foreseeable material financial effects on Thomas Crane and Trucking under Government Code Section 87103(d). Additionally, Councilman Thomas must disqualify himself from decisions affecting Huntington Beach Company because it is a source of income over $250 to him under Section 87103(c).
sections.parsing_notes: Format: ocr; LLM: Document includes OCR artifacts and formatting issues. Contains both the incoming request letter (dated December 14, 1982) and FPPC response letter (dated January 25, 1983), as well as an intermediate city memorandum. Response letter is clear on disqualification requirements. Some OCR errors in original text but key conclusions are legible.

embedding.summary: FPPC advice letter responding to Huntington Beach's request regarding conflict of interest issues arising from Councilman Pattinson being an employee of a company wholly owned by Councilman Thomas, which does business with a major local developer. The FPPC concludes that both officials must disqualify themselves from decisions with material financial effects on the Thomas company, and Thomas must also abstain from decisions affecting Huntington Beach Company.
